data.title: 16 of 23 REIT stocks beat Wall Street revenue views this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Earnings season remained active this week for the real estate sector, with 23 companies in the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) reporting quarterly results.

The XLRE delivered a weekly price return of 0.95%, nearly in line with the broader S&P 500 (SP500), which returned 0.96% during the same period. However, on a YTD basis, XLRE has gained 10.01%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 5.31% return.

The sector continued to benefit from the unchanged rate-cut decision, resilient commercial property demand, and growing investor interest in data centers and communications infrastructure.

In this week’s earnings recap, out of the 23 companies that reported results, 14 surpassed EPS estimates, four missed expectations, and five reported in line with consensus forecasts.

On the revenue side, 16 companies topped estimates, while seven missed Wall Street expectations. Compared with last year, 16 companies reported higher EPS, while 20 companies posted revenue growth.

Below are the key quarterly reports from the top five REIT companies this week:

Equinix (EQIX): The company's shares ended nearly flat after Q1 results missed estimates despite stronger guidance. Adjusted FFO rose 12% YoY to $10.79 per share, below the $11.04 estimate, while revenue grew 10% to $2.44B, missing the $2.52B estimate. However, the company raised its FY26 revenue outlook to $10.14B–$10.24B and adjusted FFO guidance to $42.31–$43.11. Q2 revenue guidance of $2.57B–$2.61B also came in ahead of estimates.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE): The stock fell ~11% after Q1 earnings reflected ongoing tenant-related pressure. Adjusted FFO came in in line at $1.73 per share but declined from last year’s $2.30. Revenue dropped to $671M, below the $695.6M consensus. The company narrowed its 2026 FFO guidance to $6.30–$6.50 per share while stating that its 2026 outlook includes a potential $25M–$30M hit from tenant wind-downs.

Iron Mountain (IRM): The stock surged ~10% after strong Q1 results and raised 2026 guidance. Q1 adjusted FFO came in at $1.43 per share, beating the $1.40 consensus. The company raised full-year adjusted FFO guidance to $5.79–$5.86 per share and revenue outlook to $7.825B–$7.925B. Q2 revenue guidance of around $1.965B also came in above expectations. Strong demand across storage and data center operations continued to support growth.

VICI Properties (VICI): The stock rose ~2% after reporting in-line Q1 results and reaffirming its 2026 outlook. Adjusted FFO came in at $0.61 per share, matching estimates, while revenue was $1.02B, also in line with consensus. The company now expects 2026 AFFO between $2.665B and $2.695B, or $2.44–$2.47 per diluted share.

American Tower (AMT): The stock rose ~2% after beating Q1 estimates. Adjusted FFO came in at $2.84 per share versus $2.75 expected, while revenue rose to $2.74B from $2.56B last year and topped the $2.65B consensus. Property revenue grew 6.8% YoY, and the REIT raised its 2026 adjusted FFO guidance to $10.90–$11.07 per share and lifted its property revenue outlook.

For the coming week, REIT stocks that are set to report their financial results are Healthpeak Properties (DOC), Host Hotels & Resorts (HST), and Realty Income (O).

data.title: Bulls vs. Bears: Reviewing POET’s fall from grace
data.description:
After more than a twofold rise in value last week, POET Technologies (POET) closed nearly 50% lower on Monday after disclosing a contract loss at the hands of Celestial AI, which was acquired by Marvell Technology (MRVL) earlier this year.

Seeking Alpha analysts reviewed the Canadian company as its stock returned to the level it was trading at before the recent rally, attributed to excitement over POET's AI photonics solutions, including speculation over an expanded purchase order from Celestial AI.

The Bulls

“Four days of under-the-table information about the contract termination creates serious legal risks for POET Technologies,” wrote SA analyst Dmytro Lebid in “POET Technologies: Why I'm Still Bullish Despite The Marvell Drama And Legal Risks.” “The severing of ties may be a tactical move by Marvell to acquire a unique technology at a discount.”

“Stock now trades near $1.1B market cap versus ~$430M cash, with ~40% valuation supported by balance sheet strength,” added Yiannis Zourmpanos in “POET: I Called The Surge, Now Reset.” “Over 80M shares traded during crash versus ~10M average, signaling capitulation and potential short-term tradable bottom formation.”

The Bears

“With a single relevant $5M+ order until now, POET faces extreme uncertainty after losing a key Marvell order due to confidentiality breaches, impacting its commercial momentum,” noted Fundamental Options in “POET Technologies: High Double-Digit Annualized Return, Lower Price Target, Rating Downgrade.”  “Despite a strong cash position post-offering, POET's estimated 24% CAGR is insufficient to justify a Buy given its early-stage risk profile.”

“POET is an extremely high-beta stock and one that could continue to see speculation if the market remains high on AI networking stocks,” opined Kumquat Research in “POET Technologies Stock: Sell Before This Goes Lower.” “I have a hard time seeing the stock rebound from this downward momentum and souring investor sentiment anytime soon and see further potential downside ahead with minimal potential upside.”

“The termination stemmed from POET's CFO disclosing confidential information, severely damaging credibility with Tier-1 partners and jeopardizing future contracts,” argued SA Investing Group Leader Oakoff Investments in “I Won't Buy POET Technologies' Dip (Rating Downgrade).” “Despite the stock's steep decline, market projections remain overly optimistic; I expect continued share price pressure and recommend avoiding POET.”

data.title: OPEC+ said to have agreed in principle to June quota hike
data.description:
Seven OPEC+ countries have provisionally agreed to raise ​oil output targets by about 188,000 barrels per day in June, a symbolic move as the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed for shipping due to Middle East tensions, Reuters reported, citing sources familiar with the matter.

According to two sources familiar with OPEC+’s plans, the decision is set to be finalized at a policy meeting of seven members of the alliance—Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, Algeria, Kazakhstan, Russia, and Oman—scheduled for Sunday.

While OPEC+ represents 21 oil-producing nations, including Iran, only the seven nations, plus the UAE, which left the group on Friday, were involved in finalizing monthly production decisions.

It will be roughly similar to a 206,000-bpd output hike OPEC+ agreed to for May after adjusting for contributions from the UAE, the sources said, requesting anonymity as they are barred from speaking to the media.

The output hike will remain symbolic until the Strait of Hormuz, a key maritime route that handled nearly 20% of global oil and LNG shipments before the Iran war, reopens and oil supplies resume from the Persian Gulf.

The output hike will mark OPEC+'s third consecutive monthly increase, signaling a business-as-usual approach from the group despite geopolitical uncertainties and the UAE's exit.

Related tickers: (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM), (NG1:COM), (XB1:COM), (XLE), (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Warren Buffett says it's important to know which deals not to take
data.description:
"The world is full of people offering things for you to do. The key is to find the ones worth doing," Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) Chairman Warren Buffett said Saturday.

"I think it's all working," Buffett said, referring to the company's operations on the sidelines of Berkshire's annual meeting. "It isn't the ideal (environment) for deploying capital."

Buffett chose Greg Abel to succeed him as CEO because he's "very, very smart" about managing businesses, he said.

Buffett is still involved in the company's investment decisions, he said in an interview with CNBC's Becky Quick.

He acknowledged that he has not learned "new industries for some years."

"We have never had more people in a gambling mood than now," he said. "That doesn't mean the investing environment is terrible."

"There's more money around than ever," Buffett said. 

"The time to buy things is when nobody else will answer their phones," he said. "When markets are collapsing, nobody answers their phones."

He said opportunities for investments will "come out of the blue." Likewise, in looking at the macro view, "it's the things you don't think about that will get you."

In commenting about Jerome Powell staying on the Federal Reserve Board as a governor, Buffett said he feels better "when he's there" than when he's not.

Regarding AI and deep fakes, "it's scary," Buffett said.

The U.S. has a “secret sauce” in its success. "Is there any other country that after a couple hundred years people still want to immigrate to?" he asked. "It has worked."

"That doesn't mean that we can't do better," he added.

"If the whole world lived by the golden rule, that would be a much better society," he said, referring to the ethic of "do unto others as you would have done to yourself."

data.title: Duffy says low-cost carriers don’t need bailouts for now
data.description:
The Trump administration doesn’t need to offer financial lifelines for low-cost carriers for now, U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said on Saturday after Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ) shut down following an unsuccessful attempt to secure a government bailout.

His remarks followed a meeting in Washington, D.C., this week during which chief executives from several low-cost airlines reportedly sought $2.5B in federal assistance, citing higher fuel and labor costs as well as low pricing power.

“I don’t think it’s necessary,” Duffy said during a press conference at Newark Liberty International Airport. “They do have access to cash. If they want to come to the U.S. government, we would be a lender of last resort,” he added.

Earlier in the day, ultralow-cost carrier Spirit Airlines began to wind down operations after its bondholders and the government failed to agree on a $500M rescue package offered by the Trump administration.

According to Duffy, some other airlines considered a possible bailout for Spirit (FLYYQ) as a chance to receive money “not necessarily based on need, but based on opportunity.”

“In the end, this was a creditor issue,” he said, adding, “If they can find dollars in the private markets—I think that’s better for them.”

U.S. low-cost carriers include Frontier Group (ULCC), JetBlue Airways (JBLU), Allegiant Travel Company (ALGT), and Sun Country Airlines (SNCY).

data.title: Real estate stocks bounce back to gains in April
data.description:
Real estate stocks bounced back to gains in April after recording a loss for the month of March.

So far in 2026, the sector is strongly in the positive territory.

April saw the broader markets deliver positive returns on the back of a stronger-than-expected March jobs report, a rise in nonfarm payrolls, some ceasefire hopes in the Iran war during the second and third weeks, a strong earnings season, and positive economic news.

The Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting, but forward guidance implied an easing bias.

The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) closed the month largely flat.

Mortgage rates fell for three straight weeks in April, while mortgage demand was stable.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) increased 8.61% month-over-month in April to 282.37 points, while the accompanying State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) was up 8.55% to $44.32.

In April, the Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (REIT:IND) added 9.04%, while the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index advanced 8.86%.

Weekly Winners & Losers

Among largecap gainers, Iron Mountain (IRM) topped the list, advancing 9.62% W/W to close at $127.19.

The document storage and data center REIT's Q1 results exceeded its expectations, fueled by growth across its businesses, and the company boosted 2026 guidance.

AvalonBay Communities (AVB) followed with a 6.42% addition to $183.45. The apartment REIT benefited after its Q1 earnings beat analyst estimates, and a media report said the company held exploratory talks about merging with Equity Residential (EQR).

Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL) (-6.89% W/W to $315.24) led the losers in the category even as the real estate services provider posted a Q1 top- and bottom-line beat.

CoStar Group (CSGP) (-4.72% W/W to $34.72) and CBRE Group (CBRE) (-4.37% W/W to $141.81) followed. The week saw CBRE price a $750M offering of 5.250% senior notes due 2036 and CoStar post mixed quarterly earnings.

For the midcap gainers, OUTFRONT Media (OUT) (+5.63% W/W to $31.71) and Kilroy Realty (KRC) (+5.52% W/W to $33.64) topped the list. Kilroy Realty was gaining on the back of a quarterly earnings beat.

The St. Joe Company (JOE) (-13.05% W/W to $61.74) topped the losers. The company posted Q1 earnings this week.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) followed with a 12.68% retreat to $41.39 amid lower first-quarter earnings on the back of potential tenant wind-downs.

For smallcap stocks, RE/MAX Holdings (RMAX) (+34.92% W/W to $10.78) led the gainers. The stock gained as Real Brokerage (REAX) confirmed the acquisition of RE/MAX for an enterprise value of ~$880M.

Two Harbors Investment (TWO) followed with a 14.00% gain to $12.54. The week saw the company post its Q1 earnings and announce an amended merger agreement with CrossCountry Mortgage.

Adamas Trust (ADAM) (+11.82% W/W to $8.89) was a notable gainer on the back of its Q1 earnings that beat the consensus by a wide margin, helped by gains from derivatives.

Ironically, REAX topped the losers, retreating 20.52% to $2.13.

Hotel101 Global Holdings (HBNB) was also a significant decliner, shedding 10.83% to close at $6.01.

data.title: Earnings Scorecard: 12 out of 15 S&P 500 consumer discretionary firms beat EPS estimates this week
data.description:
Earnings season gathered pace this week, with a busy stretch that saw 15 companies in the S&P 500’s consumer discretionary sector (XLY) report quarterly results.

Year to date, State Street Consumer Disc Sel Sect SPDR ETF (XLY) has fallen 0.6%, underperforming the broader S&P 500 index's nearly 6% gains over the same period.

The sector’s performance this quarter reflects persistent inflation concerns, rising Treasury yields, and escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, which pushed oil prices above $100 per barrel. Higher energy costs weighed on household spending power and increased input and shipping costs, pressuring consumer-facing businesses.

In this week’s earnings recap, out of the 15 companies that reported, 12 surpassed profit estimates, two fell short of expectations, and one reported in line. On the revenue side, 12 companies exceeded estimates, while three missed.

On a year-over-year basis, 12 of 15 companies reported EPS growth, while 13 posted revenue gains.

Below are the latest quarterly reports from five notable companies in the consumer discretionary sector this week:

Domino's Pizza (DPZ) reported revenue was up 3.6% year over year to $1.15B for Q1, missing the consensus estimate by $10M. International stores saw growth of 4.0%, while U.S. stores generated 2.8% growth. Same-store sales were 1.5% higher at U.S. company-owned stores and 0.8% higher for franchised locations. While both those marks missed the consensus estimate of analysts, the pizza chain noted that Q1 was another quarter of positive order count and market share growth for Domino's in the U.S.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) posted positive comparable sales growth in Q1. Total revenue was up 7.4% year over year to $3.1B. The increase was driven by new restaurant openings and a 0.5% increase in comparable restaurant sales due to higher transactions of 0.6%, partially offset by a 0.1% decrease in average check. The comparable sales growth mark was better than the consensus estimate of -0.9%.

Starbucks (SBUX) in its second-quarter earnings report, recorded its third straight quarter of positive comparable sales after a streak of seven quarters in negative territory. Global comparable store sales were up 6.2% to easily top the +3.7% consensus estimate, driven by a 3.8% increase in comparable transactions and a 2.3% increase in average ticket. U.S. comparable store sales were up 7.2% during the quarter, driven by a 4.3% gain in comparable transactions and a 2.7% increase in average ticket. The U.S. comparable sales tally topped the consensus expectation for a gain of 3.7%.

Ford Motor (F) posted better-than-expected first-quarter revenue and profit results, but that was eclipsed by a multi-billion-dollar loss in its EV segment and $1.9B in negative free cash flow.

Amazon (AMZN)  beat estimates in its Q1 results. AWS was a standout, with revenue up 28% to $37.6B, its fastest growth in 15 quarters, helping drive total company operating income to $23.9B. The main sticking point was heavy AI-related spending, which led to free cash flow falling sharply to $1.2B over the trailing 12 months as capex surged. For Q2, the company guided for $194B to $199B in sales and $20B to $24B in operating income.

For the upcoming week, Airbnb (ABNB), McDonald's (MCD), and DoorDash (DASH) are among the key companies in the sector scheduled to report quarterly results.

Consumer Discretionary ETFs: (XLY), (VCR), (FXD), (FDIS), (RSPD), and (RXI)

data.title: Anthropic reportedly in talks to buy AI chips from U.K. startup Fractile
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) has recently held discussions with Fractile to buy the U.K. startup’s inference chips designed to run AI models more efficiently, The Information reported on Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.

According to two people who have spoken to Fractile leaders, the maker of the Claude artificial intelligence models has been in talks to buy the London-based firm’s chips when they become available next year.

Fractile, founded in 2022, is competing with startups such as Groq and Cerebras (CBRS) to develop server chips designed to offer better efficiency compared to Nvidia (NVDA) graphics processing units.

In December, Nvidia (NVDA) reached a non-exclusive deal with Groq to license its inference technology, adding the company’s founder, Sunny Madra, and two other employees as part of the agreement.

In February, Fractile announced plans to invest £100M ($137M) to bolster its U.K. operations over the next three years.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway CEO Abel stresses technology opportunity at annual meeting
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) CEO Greg Abel reviewed results for the investment conglomerate's sprawling collection of businesses, noting that technology, including artificial intelligence, "touches the whole franchise of Berkshire."

The new CEO emphasized the importance of leveraging technology to fuel growth. At some point, management realized, "We're going to be a builder of technology, rather than just a buyer of technology," he said in his first annual meeting as CEO.

In terms of AI, "it has to be additive to our businesses. We're not going to have AI just to have AI," Abel said.

Earlier, the company delivered Q1 operating earnings of $11.3B, climbing 18% from the year-ago period, helped by a 29% jump in its insurance underwriting profit. Its cash pile grew 6.5% Q/Q to $397.4B.

In his discussion of Q1 results, Abel attributed part of the company's improvement in insurance underwriting profit to the $860M after-tax charge related to California wildfires the company recorded in 2025 results. By contrast, 2026 results didn't reflect any catastrophic events.

The CEO stated that its Geico business is focused on restarting growth. It has to "get the price to risk right," retain customers, and grow its policies in force, he said. There's much to do there. Geico's policies in force, or PIF, rose 2% in Q1, while its closest competitor, Progressive's (PGR) PIF climbed 11%. "It's not going to be easy to just restart the growth engine," he said.

Abel also mentioned Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.B) $1.8B investment in Tokio Marine (TKOMY) (TKOMF), calling it a long-term strategic partnership. 

The company's railroad, BNSF, has made progress, but there's "a lot more to be done," he said, pointing to the unit's operating margin expansion to 34.4% in Q1 from 32.1% in last year's Q1. 

"We see a lot of opportunity here to become better," he added. "That's going to require a step-change in how we approach our operations." Technology will be key, he said, as some competitors have gapped up its use.

"How can we move our cars quicker and meet our customer expectations?" he asks. That also requires determining the best use of its resources and employees. 

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) Energy has made progress in its litigation position. In April, the Oregon Court of Appeals ruled in favor of its PacifiCorp utility, which allowed the company to recover about $1B of securities it had posted, Abel said. "We have reset the stage," he added.

Abel pointed out that the company's net cash and short-term securities are $380.2B, which is less than the headline number of $397.4B. The difference is in $17.2B payable for purchases of U.S. Treasury bills, he said.

The CEO said its Occidental Chemical purchase resulted in a little more than a $3B decrease in results. Still, he's “very pleased” with the transaction.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Earnings scoreboard: 11 of 12 consumer staple names beat earnings estimates this week
data.description:
Consumer staples companies delivered a broadly positive and resilient set of earnings last week, with pricing power continuing to support revenue growth even as volumes remained under pressure.

Broadly, the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) has outperformed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) YTD, rising about 8.35% versus roughly 5.6% for the broader market, driven by a clear defensive rotation to staples.

Out of 35 consumer staple names that are part of the S&P 500 (SPY), 12 reported earnings last week, with most delivering a beat on estimates. 11 out of 12 firms beat EPS estimates, while 1 reported in-line numbers.

Based on the revenue, 10 companies beat revenue estimates, while 2 companies fell short of estimates.

Of the 12 consumer companies of the S&P 500 index, 10 reported Y/Y earnings growth, and 9 saw topline growth.

Here is a list of the latest quarterly reports in the consumer staples sector this week:

Coca-Cola (KO) Q1 earnings report comfortably beat expectations across nearly every key metric, and the move higher rippled through the broader consumer staples space. Global unit case volume grew 3% during the quarter, with all segments positive.

Altria Group (MO) topped Q1 estimates with revenue up 3.2% to $5.43B and adjusted EPS at $1.32, as higher pricing offset declining shipment volumes. The gains were driven by pricing power across cigarettes and oral tobacco, which helped counter ongoing volume declines amid weak consumer demand.

Colgate-Palmolive (CL) topped consensus estimates of analysts with its Q1 earnings report. Its revenue was up 8.4% to $5.32B. Non-GAAP EPS was reported at $0.97 vs. $0.94 consensus and $0.91 a year ago. The company said its leadership in toothpaste continued with its global market share at 41.1. YTD, while its leadership in manual toothbrushes continued with its global market share at 32.6% YTD.

Hershey (HSY) - The chocolate-maker set a profit guidance for the year with a midpoint that is less than Wall Street’s estimates, though it beat Q1 estimates. In its candy, mint, and gum segment, retail takeaway increased 8.1% despite higher prices, although Hershey’s market share declined Y/Y due to increased competition and timing of merchandising programs.

Shares of Clorox (CLX) came under pressure after the company’s fiscal Q3 results and outlook for the year suggested continued headwinds for the consumer products company. It's Non-GAAP EPS of $1.64 beat by $0.10, while its topline was at $1.67B, in line with estimates, but showed no growth Y/Y.

Mondelez International (MDLZ) saw Q1 organic sales jump 3%, beating estimates, with revenue up ~8%, driven by strong growth in emerging markets. However, adjusted EPS fell 14.9% Y/Y to $0.67, missing expectations, as margin pressure from higher input costs offset pricing gains.

At the industry level, 4 household products, 3 food products, 2 beverages, 1 personal care product, 1 tobacco, and 1 distribution & retail company reported earnings this week.

Among the consumer staple names that are reporting earnings next week are Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), Kenvue (KVUE), Kraft Heinz (KHC), and Tyson Foods (TSN).
